title: LoL Esports |
content:
We’re delighted that we can finally reveal that the culmination to the LEC season, the LEC Summer Finals, is coming to Nice, France!

Which team will earn the final spot up for grabs at Worlds this year and take a shot at international glory? The answer awaits in Nice.

We will be welcoming our passionate community to the Palais Nikaia in Nice from September 18 to 20. The capital of the French Riviera will be taken over by the top LEC teams and players, as well as your favourite broadcast talent, co-streamers and of course, thousands of our beloved LEC fans ready to cheer on their team. The three-day League of Legends extravaganza will be an unforgettable experience.

Here’s everything you need to know about the most exciting weekend in the LEC calendar!

The entire season is building up to this moment as we not only crown our Summer champion but also find out who will secure the third and final spot to represent the LEC at World’s in North America in October.

Last year’s Summer Finals in Madrid was spectacular! The fans, the show, and the energy, were truly next level. We’re excited to do it all again while continuing to evolve and deliver the best fan experience possible.

title: HLE Kanavi: "We lost pretty heavily to BLG, but if we meet again in the final, I think we can win"
summary: HLE Kanavi: "We lost pretty heavily to BLG, but if we meet again in the final, I think we can win"  sheepesports.com
content:
Your premier source for esports news, exclusive leaks, and expert analysis. Stay ahead of the game with the latest updates from League of Legends, Valorant, CS2, and Rocket League.

HLE jungler Kanavi reflects on the 3-1 loss to BLG at MSI 2026, what froze his team in games one and two, and why he believes HLE can win a finals rematch.

I enjoy making content that drives my passion for esports. Also the creator of this website !

title: G2 Esports proves that T1 has an over-reliance problem
summary: G2 Esports proves that T1 has an over-reliance problem  WIN.GG
content:
The MSI match between G2 Esports and T1 proved that the LCK powerhouse needs to take a good look at its strategy.

For years now, the LCK has been a dominant force in the competitive League of Legends space. While the LCP has occasionally come close to the number one spot, that hasn’t been the case for long.

In particular, T1 is easily the biggest name in the scene, especially with the team having six World Championships under its belt.

However, the League of Legends meta is constantly shifting. Names such as Faker and Oner have definitely adapted to the ever-changing meta better than most, but the match against G2 Esports during the 2026 MSI lower bracket suggests that T1 may have more underlying issues than expected.

Despite what most fans predicted, G2 Esports displayed a masterclass performance against T1, knocking the team out of MSI.

Going into the match, G2 was the definite underdog, peresports prediction markets. The team hasn’t beaten T1 in a best-of-five in seven years, and there was little reason to believe that this would change here. However, the first match alone set the pace for what was to come.

title: EWC League Of Legends 2026: What You Need To Know
summary: EWC League Of Legends 2026: What You Need To Know  Hotspawn
content:
The Esports World Cup (EWC) League of Legends tournament is returning in 2026. Taking place from July 15 to July 19 in Paris, 16 teams will be competing for the largest share of the $2,000,000 prize pool.

Coming shortly after the end of MSI 2026, the top teams from around the world will have another opportunity to prove themselves. After Gen.G won in 2025, who will rise up this year?

Even though the EWC League of Legends tournament just lasts five days this year, we will actually see more teams competing in this year’s edition. Winning will not only guarantee a higher prize pool but also allow organizations to earnEWC Club Championshippoints, which could mean even more money for the teams.

EWC League of Legends 2026 will run from July 15 to July 19, featuring two stages: Group Stage and the Playoffs.

The group stage will see four groups of four teams compete in a GSL-style double-elimination format, with the top two teams from each group advancing to playoffs. Most matches will be best-of-one, while elimination matches will be best-of-three.

The top eight teams then qualify for playoffs, using a single-elimination bracket. Quarterfinals and semifinals will feature best-of-three series, while the Grand Final will be a best-of-five. The16 teamsare currently split as follows:

title: "We can break even and be self-sustaining within Riot" – The firm behind League of Legends is "super close" to making esports sustainable
summary: "We can break even and be self-sustaining within Riot" – The firm behind League of Legends is "super close" to making esports sustainable &nbs...
content:
Riot Games' publishing and esports boss, John Needham, on the journey to profitability, Riot's transmedia ambitions, and why it allowed gambling sponsorship

Riot Games' esports business still isn't profitable.

This has been the case for a long time, and historically it hasn'tnecessarilybeen a problem for the League of Legends giant; at various points in the past, then-head of esports John Needham explained that his firmwanted to ensure the broader ecosystem, including the various esports teams, was profitable. But the aim was for esports to be a sustainable business in its own right.

That business is now "super close" to being profitable. What's changed? Needham –now head of publishing and esports at Riot– says the firm has adjusted how it operates its pro-gaming business since 2020.

"Coming out of the pandemic, we were in this esports winter," he tellsGamesIndustry.biz.

"The sponsorship business was weak, teams were having a hard time getting capital and investment in their business. We pivoted our business model away from sponsorships to digital items; that was a big positive move for teams. Teams also adjusted how much they were spending. The team business is in as good a place as it has ever been, now, and we are trying to do other things to allow teams to make alternate sources of revenue."

title: Mid-Season Invitational 2026: LEC's hopes dashed as Lyon knocks out G2 Esports
summary: Mid-Season Invitational 2026: LEC's hopes dashed as Lyon knocks out G2 Esports  Gamereactor UK
content:
Well, that does it for the LEC's hopes in the Mid-Season Invitational tournament in South Korea. The Lower Bracket Round 3 match has just come to a close and it has seen Lyon dispatch G2 Esports in a rather convincing manner, with a 3-0 result going in favour of the LCS organisation.

This result means that there are now only two games left at MSI 2026, with the Lower Bracket Final to be played tomorrow, July 11, between Hanwha Life and Lyon, the former securing a place afterlosing the Upper Bracket Final to Bilibili Gamingyesterday.

After this match, the winner will advance to the Grand Finals to face Bilibili Gaming on Sunday, July 14, where the victor will secure a Worlds 2026 spot and also walk away with $500,000 in prize money.

With this all being said, you can see the fixtures for the days ahead below.

Lower Bracket Final - July 11 at 9:00 BST/10:00 CEST

Grand Final - July 12 at 7:00 BST/8:00 CEST
